description:
You are working as a data-scientist on a small data-cleaning pipeline.  
Inside the working directory “/home/user/datasets” there is already a CSV file called “raw.csv”.  
The goal is to ① eliminate every data row that contains at least one empty field, and ② record very small metadata about what you did, so that automated validation can later decide whether the cleaning step was successful.

When you are done, the filesystem **must** contain exactly the two new artefacts listed below (no more, no less):

1. /home/user/datasets/cleaned.csv  
   • CSV header must be preserved unmodified.  
   • Only rows whose every column is non-empty must be present.  
   • Field order and the comma separator must stay unchanged.  
   • There must be a final trailing newline after the last row.

2. /home/user/datasets/processing.log  
   • This log file must contain exactly two lines and nothing else.  
   • Line 1:  “Input rows: N” – where N is the total number of **data** rows that were in raw.csv (do not count the header).  
   • Line 2:  “Output rows: M” – where M is the number of data rows written to cleaned.csv.  
   • The two lines must appear in that exact order, each terminated by a single newline character (the last line may end with a newline as well).

If the cleaning step cannot be completed for any reason, create an additional file called “/home/user/datasets/error.log” containing a single line starting with the word “ERROR: ” and a short description of the problem.  
However, if the pipeline finishes successfully, **/home/user/datasets/error.log must not exist**.

Constraints / hints:
• Keep the solution short—3 – 4 shell commands is enough.  
• Use only standard POSIX utilities that are present in a minimal Linux installation (e.g., awk, grep, wc, head, tail, etc.).  
• Do not modify, rename or delete the original raw.csv file.  
• Make sure the target files are created with default user permissions; no root access is available.

After you believe everything is correct you may display (cat) the two newly created files so the user can visually confirm, but remember that the automated grader will rely solely on the exact file paths and content rules described above.
truth:
Existing state BEFORE the agent starts:

Directory structure:
 /home/user/
 └── datasets/
     └── raw.csv

Content of /home/user/datasets/raw.csv (including final newline):

id,name,age,score
1,Alice,30,85
2,Bob,,90
3,Charlie,25,
4,,27,88
5,David,22,92

Ground-truth values expected AFTER successful task completion:

1. /home/user/datasets/cleaned.csv  (including final newline)

id,name,age,score
1,Alice,30,85
5,David,22,92

2. /home/user/datasets/processing.log  (including final newline)

Input rows: 5
Output rows: 2

3. /home/user/datasets/error.log must NOT exist.

Additional verification notes for the grader:
• cleaned.csv must have exactly 3 lines (header + 2 data lines) and each line must end with \n.  
• processing.log must have exactly 2 lines; no leading/trailing spaces except the single space after the colon shown above.  
• File paths are case-sensitive.  Missing files or extra files (except typical editor backup files like *.swp) should cause the task to fail.
